"If you say soâ€¦" Eta began to recant the visions she'd been having to catch Chester up to speed. Then the most recent vision, detail for detail, as best as she could remember it.<br /><br />"You must think we're both nutsâ€¦ huh?" Eta asked.<br /><br />"Actually." Chester mused, "I find this fascinating. We've been studying this kind of phenomenon for years."<br /><br />"You have?" Eta turned to give her full attention.<br /><br />"We, as in those in the science and technology field. Not me personally." Chester pulled up his tablet, and starting scrolling through files. "I've worked on a lot of wicked crazy projects, for a lot of 'organizations'. I can say that some of them have research the kinds of experiences Ash was telling me about."<br /><br />"This oneâ€¦" Chester handed the tablet to Eta who scrolled through the site. "â€¦ for example. The Esion Eti'hw Institute &amp; Research Facility. They pronounce that second word like Et-Eew. I built some seriously intense stuff for them. They're just the kind of people who dabble in your experiences. I wouldn't use them though, they give me the heebeegeebees. Especially that main guyâ€¦ what's his name.Thaddeus. I finally turned the project over to some eager MIT brats. I couldn't work with them anymore."<br /><br />"What kinds of projects? What's the research-" Eta took a quick breath. "The tower."<br /><br />"Yeah," Chester pointed to the picture, "that's their pride and joy there. Esion Tower."<br /><br />"No, you don't understand, my vision, I was at that tower last night." She recounted her vision to them.<br /><br />"The white haired man had to be this guy" Chester took the tablet, navigated to the about page, and handed it back. "Thaddeus J. B. Rotcudnoc, say that ten times fast." he laughed. "I think he said it's Slavic."<br /><br />Eta could feel the hair raise up on the back of her neck. â€œThatâ€™s him!â€<br /><br />â€œI knew itâ€¦â€ Chester said, â€œâ€¦ heebeegeebees. You two better watch yourselves if heâ€™s involved in this. Here, read the about page.â€<br /><br />Eta read opening out-loud:<br /><br />"Have you ever wondered what is just beyond this world? Have you thought there had to be more? Religion and Spiritualists have let you down? Turn to science. Our work in quantum mechanics and extra dimensional evolution, is providing solid scientific answers to many of life's oldest questions. The Esion Eti'hw Institute was founded on the ground breaking principals outlined in the thesis 'Unlocking the Extra-Normal Mind. A study of the human mindâ€™s unlocked potential', By Dr. Richard T. Briar."<br /><br />"Sounds freaky." She said, "Just like the rest of the kooks I've been reading about the last few weeks. "<br /><br />"Waitâ€¦ did you say Dr. Briar? Let me see that." Ash grabbed the tablet out her hand. He scrolled to the bottom and stopped. His brow furrowed, and lips pursed.<br /><br />"What's the problem? You look like you just drank bad milk." Eta observed.<br /><br />"The night before the attack, this guy sat across from me at the coffee shop. He was asking a bunch of weird questions about my fight with the homeless guy. It was almost as if he knew I had seen something. I just passed it off at the time. I had forgotten about him until just now."<br /><br />"So, you have strange encounter where you see something that wasn't there, theoreticallyâ€¦" Chester interjected, "â€¦ then this guy shows up asking questions. Then that night you are attacked by the 'League of Assassins'. Then last night, Eta has a Vision of Thaddeus at the tower. Yep, I knew that place gave me the creeps, and you two are in it deep."<br /><br />Eta could feel her skin tighten, and heat rising up in her throat. "So what does this mean?"<br /><br />"I have no idea, but I need to get some answers. I'm going to do some field reconnaissance. You two be alright for a few hours?"<br /><br />"Sure," Eta said. "I'll clean my guns. I have a feeling I'll be needing them."<br /><br />"Cool! You can set all of that up here on the counter. I'll start checking too, see what they've been up to since I stopped working with them. See?â€ Chester grinned, â€œI knew I was here for a reason."<br /><br />He's cute, a little young for her taste. Well... He's not that young. Shake it off girl...<br /><br /><br /><br />Reconnaissance<br /><br /><br /><br />Ash rode the Vulcan to a military surplus store, and restocked a few items he thought he would need. He also picked up two uniforms to match the security in the website photos. He threw them into the saddle bag, and hopped on. The purr of the motor, and vibration running through his body was calming, as all bike rides were. He turned west onto the I-10, then headed north on PCH up to Malibu.<br /><br />Ash could not get the image of the large man behind Chester out of his mind. The things he often saw on people, the medication he'd been taking for hallucinations. Now it turns out he might not be crazy after all? Or maybe he still was. Nothing has been right since he left. 'Maybe I'm still in bed in Oregon', but he shook it off. This was no dream.<br /><br />After a few turns on less populated roads, he arrived at the Esion Eti'hw Institute &amp; Research Facility, Malibu, CA. The property was large, with several buildings in the center, and a long drive from the front gate. One large tower dominating the rest. They weren't just going to let him in, so he drove past the front gate, and parked down the road, behind a city welcome sign.<br /><br />He worked his way through brush and sand hills, and plopped down on a hill near some of the buildings. He saw hundreds of people coming and going throughout the facility.<br /><br />Then he sensed he too was being watched.<br /><br />Ash put down his binoculars and turned his head to the left. A large man, one of the two from the alley they met Chester, was laying next to him. He was dressed in all white, with a goofy grin on his face. He nodded and stood up, then motioned for Ash to follow. They walked right up to the edge of the gate, and the man stepped through the fence as though it were not solid. He motioned for Ash to follow.<br /><br />Ash tentatively put his hand out, and pushed right through the fence.<br /><br />The man walked right up to a guard that was walking by, and purposefully stood in his way. The look on his face was that of a child playing a game. The man walked through him, and put his hand out to Ash. He couldn't hear him, and he didn't open his mouth, but he felt the man say "After you".<br /><br />They walked together, inspected labs, took a trip into the elevator, up to the CEO's office on the top floor. The two continued to walk side by side, people moving right through them. The large man was always at ease, but never spoke. They approached the large double doors to the office, and stopped.<br /><br />A plaque was hung prominently on the red oak, "Thaddeus J. B. Rotcudnoc", Chief Executive Officer.<br /><br />Ash looked to his guide for direction, and with a waive of his hand, he understood "After you.". Ash tried to open the door knob, but his hand went through the door, so he half stepped half fell through the doorway. After regaining his balance, he shot a glance back to his silent companion, who was belly laughing, bent over, and pointing at him.<br /><br />"Funny..." He shot back. "Michael? Gabriel?"<br /><br />The Guide stood a little straighter, big smile, but shook his head no.<br /><br />"Fine. I shall call you..." Ash searched for a fitting name. "Bob. That way I can say 'Bob did it' and actually be talking about someone."<br /><br />The Guide cocked his head to one side, and somewhat to Ash's surprise, he gave an enthusiastic nod to the affirmative, and smiled again.<br /><br />"OK Bob, what are we doing here." &nbsp;Ash followed his gaze.<br /><br />The office was large, bigger than most homes. Book shelves lined the walls on both sides, a conference area was set up front. In the back, behind a half wall, was a desk.<br /><br />"That desk may as well have been a dining room table in it's former life." Ash said.<br /><br />The two of them walked through to the back of the office, where a tall thin man, in a tailored suit, was talking into the window. His tuft of white hair, nearly glowed in the reflection. A blue tooth perched perfectly upon his ear complimented his square jaw. He was an older man, but fit.<br /><br />He was not just accompanied by a single creature, as with so many others, he was nearly covered in them. They moved in and out of him. A mist covered his ankles, and two creatures, larger than the others, stood by each side. Thaddeus may not have noticed Ash's presence, but the two creatures were not pleased.<br /><br />Each was nearly 9 foot tall, with the head of a bull, and white paint swiped across their chest. They snorted at Bob, who returned with a smile and waive, not in the least concerned. Ash backed a little closer to Bob, followed him to the desk, and stood by the window on the opposite side of the room.<br /><br />Thaddeus wrapped up his conversation, and sat at the desk. He put his hand on the far edge, feeling the groves in the design.<br /><br />He paused. Turned back to the window. Stared at Ashâ€¦<br /><br />Ash felt a cold tinge run through his spine. Could Thaddeus see him?<br /><br />He sniffed the airâ€¦ tilted his headâ€¦ Shook his head and sighed.<br /><br />No. He was staring through Ash.<br /><br />Turning back to this desk, Thaddeus inserted his pen into a small depression just to the edge of the desk. A door revealed itself in the side panel, and inside was a notebook. Thaddeus's snow white hair fell out of place as he bent down to pull it out. He stopped a moment to fix it in a mirror that was stationed prominently on his desk.<br /><br />Then, Bob walked out of the window, took ten steps on thin air, turned, and motioned for Ash to follow. Despite the oddity of the experience thus far, Ash felt his legs and arms cramp up at the thought of walking out of the 13th floor window.<br /><br />Tentatively, he pushed his hand through the glass. As with the door, and gate, it went straight through.<br /><br />Bob waited, arms folded.<br /><br />Was that a smirk on his face?<br /><br />Ash took a half step through the glass, and found the air to be solid just outside. Taking a deep breath, he closed his eyes, and put his full weight on the foot, then took three more steps with eyes closed. When he opened his eyes, they were standing on nothing.<br /><br />Bob took Ash's hand, and then they floated, slowly down to the ground, stopping just shy of it. Floating ten feet above the crowds head, they walked back to the motorcycle. The last steps were like stairs, back to the ground floor.<br /><br />Bob smiled again, and with a bow, a bright light flashed, blinding Ash for a moment. When his eyes adjusted, Bob was gone. Somehow, Ash felt that he wasn't really gone. He turned to continue the direction he'd ridden on the way in, so as not to pass the guard gate twice.<br /><br />Turning off of Las Virgenes Rd, he hooked back to LA on the 101. He passed a Denny's on the way out of town, which reminded him how hungry he was. Eta and Chester were never going to believe this, Ash wasn't sure if he believed.<br /><br /><br /><br />The Orphanage<br /><br /><br /><br />"I have an appointment to make, and this program will take a few hours to run." Chester took his last sip of Mountain Dew, and threw the bottle in the trash can. "You are welcome to stay here and get some rest. Or, you can come along if you'd like."<br /><br />Eta glanced at the code running in the background. "Sure" With a nod, she left a note for Ash, and they grabbed their coats and headed out the door.<br /><br />In the car Chester rambled on and on about this Orphanage they were going to. "Ms. Wu is one of the most inspiring people I've ever met. She was raised in mainland China. She was a second child, but her parents refused to abort her, so she lived without honor or privilege. Eventually, when she was 15, he parents sent her to the USA hoping for a better life. She was mistreated by her "tour guides", but eventually broke free. Now she runs an Orphanage here in L.A."<br /><br />Chester nodded to the building. It was a nice place for being an Orphanage. It looked more like a private university campus. "She takes the ones that no one else will take. Many of her kids will spend the rest of their childhoods here, some the rest of their lives."<br /><br />They parked, and walked in. The entrance was beautiful, laid with quality white tile, and a marble receptionistâ€™s desk.<br /><br />"Hey Elaine", Chester waived to the young lady, who couldn't have been more than 17.<br /><br />She nodded, and smiled, but then turned back to her telephone. "Yes sir, and what is the address?"<br /><br />Chester led her to the common room. A library ran the length of one wall, with seating areas to read. There were cubicle style desks with computers, and a separate room to the side for games.<br /><br />"Hey Chester" A young black girl waived from the computer area. "Whaz up?"<br /><br />"Jazmin my young protege," Chester walked over to her "Are you giving Ms. Wu a hard time?"<br /><br />"Me? Never." she said with obvious sarcasm. "I swear, the computer crashes are not my fault this time. I even tried to fix it, but it's a nasty virus. Worse I've seen yet."<br /><br />She was an older kid, 17 maybe. Bright blues and purples were weaved into her jet black braids. A thousand ribbons waiving in the wind when she moved her head. She was &nbsp;fit young lady, although it was hard to tell for sure under loose jeans and a t-shirt. Eta hardly noticed at first, but her right arm was skinnier than her left. Same length, but half the diameter.<br /><br />"Who's the chick?" She asked Chester, and when she looked up Eta was taken back by piercing gray eyes, almost silver.<br /><br />"She's a friend, Eta".<br /><br />"Hm. She safe?"<br /><br />"She's safer than you know, or need. Why? What kind of trouble are you into now?"<br /><br />"Nothing big, but this guy has me worried a bit."<br /><br />Chester and Eta looked down at her screen. Black boxes, with green letters. Chat room. "People still use these? I haven't seen a chat room since 1997."<br /><br />"It's the hackers preferred method of communication. Anonymity is top priority." Chester said, turning to Jazmin, "Hey, is that an ARK room?"<br /><br />"Yes sir it is"<br /><br />"I thought I told you to stay out away from ARK, they can be very dangerous."<br /><br />"I'm sorry, ARK?" Eta interrupted.<br /><br />"Anonymous Response Kings" Chester said. "They are a Hacktivists group."<br /><br />"Like Everyone?"<br /><br />"Yes, but more prone to serious attacks. Not just pranks."<br /><br />"Where did you get this girl?" Jazmin asked.<br /><br />"She's not a hacker, but she's safe. Trust me."<br /><br />"I don't trust anyone, that's how I stay alive. Besides," Jazmin pointed at the screen, "I'm not hacking for them, just watching for activity. Surfing for entertainment."<br /><br />Eta looked down as Jazmin's fingers ran the keyboard. "bds33s@ll" was next to the words flying across the screen.<br /><br />"So what's your problem and with who?" Chester asked.<br /><br />"This guy." Jazmin pointed to the screen, where the name appeared as "D@rkM@tt3r".<br /><br />"DarkMatter", Chester said. "That's cute."<br /><br />"Well, he's not cute. He's on the Jihad sections trying to stir up trouble makers. I've been trying to hack his firewalls, but he's got a lot security in place."<br /><br />"Just stay clear of trouble. Make sure to report anything real bad to the FBI, and stay out of it."<br /><br />"Yes Father" Jazmin rolled her eyes.<br /><br />A woman walked in from an adjacent office, "Peter." she said to a young man with a hoodie. "Did you complete your homework before turning on the game?"<br /><br />"Yes Ms. Wu."<br /><br />"Show me..."<br /><br />"Um... " the kid fidgeted, "I'll go get that started right now Ms. Wu."<br /><br />"Good boy."<br /><br />Chester pointed to the boy as he left, "That kid has been through 14 foster homes, and he's 16. There is almost no chance he could be adopted anywhere now. But Ms. Wu keeps him in line, and he's been showing great improvement, he just needed to have someone show him love and discipline."<br /><br />"How did you find this place?" Eta asked.<br /><br />"Well..." Chester smiled, "I was given a list of places I could use to fulfill community service, and I threw darts at the wall. The first one stuck on this place. What I didn't know, was how much Ms. Wu would mean to me. She treated me like one of her troubled kids, and she was just as tough on me as them."<br /><br />"Ms. Wu, Good to see you." Chester said as the woman approached.<br /><br />She was small, maybe 4'9"? It was hard to say, but she looked like she was in her late 60's.<br /><br />After some introductions, Ms. Wu turned to Eta, "Dear, I need this young man for a few minutes to fix my computers. These kids keep downloading things. Would you be willing to wait here?"<br /><br />Eta nodded in the affirmative.<br /><br />Eta walked over to the book shelves and glanced at the titles. They seemed to have everything from Honors World History to Sesame Street. Eta grabbed a book on US History that had pictures, and sat down in a nearby chair. She put her feet up on the ottoman, and leafed through the pictures.<br /><br />After a few minutes, a young girl walked by to the book shelf and sat down next to her.<br /><br />"Your pretty" she said, not looking up from her book.<br /><br />Her speech was odd. Not down syndrome, but maybe Autism, Eta thought. "Thank you" Eta replied.<br /><br />"You have dreams..." The kid said.<br /><br />A little taken back, "Yes, most people do. Do you have dreams?" trying to make conversation.<br /><br />"Yes..." not looking up from her book, "... but not like yours. Yours come true."<br /><br />Eta was not sure how to respond, she just stared at the girl for what felt like an eternity.<br /><br />"You need to stop him. That Tower Man is scary.â€ The girl stood up, and walked away with her book.<br /><br />Chester walked up behind her, â€œShe's another one that cannot be adopted, but such a brave heart when you get to know her.<br /><br />"She certainly was interesting to speak with" Eta felt a shudder run through her spine.<br /><br />"She spoke to you?"<br /><br />"Something like that."<br /><br />"You should feel privileged. She doesn't normally speak with anyone until she's known them for a few weeks." He bumped his shoulder into hers, jestingly. "I'm done here, let's get back to my place and check on the program.â€<br /><br /><br />Still Missing<br /><br /><br />Dr. Briar paced his office. He'd lost Col. Ashland in Oregon. Wild-Card, as his benefactor now called him. But more importantly, he'd lost the Problem Child again. At every turn, he was sure he'd found him, only to come up empty. His benefactor was losing his patience, and Dr. Briar was losing his usefulness. He needed something, anything, any kind of lead.<br /><br />The black screen became to fill with green words.<br /><br />Dr. Briar held his breath.<br /><br />â€œAny news on the Wild Card?â€<br /><br />He typed back, â€œAll quiet. But I still feel we could use him.â€<br /><br />â€œNo. Problem Child?â€<br /><br />â€œNo new leads.â€<br /><br />â€œDo I need to seek a replacement?â€<br /><br />â€œDr. Briar felt a chill run up his spine.â€<br /><br />â€œNo. I will figure something out. â€œ<br /><br />â€œLet's hope you do.â€<br /><br />*End Connection*<br /><br />Butterfly's in the stomach, was becoming an all too common occurrence.<br /><br />Dr. Briar knew that he would have to take risks for scientific break through, but putting his test subjects down, and now becoming a well paid well equipped bounty hunter, was not in his original plan. He wasn't fit for this work, but his expertise on the subject was needed... For the time being.<br /><br /><br /><br />Hacked<br /><br /><br /><br />Back at the house, Chester was working on one side of the counter, and Eta sat on the other side.<br /><br />"You know the problem with these MIT brats?" Chester smirked. "They are way too sure of themselves. They are so convinced that they're the smartest kid in the room, and everyone has to answer to them. They've never had to make it on their own, or do their own work. They get sloppy in their life, and sloppy in their code. And that's how I got in."<br /><br />"Chesterâ€¦ if you don't mind me askingâ€¦" Eta kept her head down clearing the barrel of her Beretta 9mm. "How does someone with your obvious talent end up teaching computers for dummies at a homeless shelter, and hanging out with orphans?<br /><br />"Well, it's a long story, but not I'm not sure it's a terribly interesting one... I was one of the youngest graduates of Caltech to date, I was working with a major IT firm all bright eyed, ready to change the world. Then I started to realize that all I was doing was making really ugly people a lot of money. So I started drinking, then came the drugs.<br /><br />Soon I couldn't code anymore so I lost my job. My parents couldn't bare the fact their son turned into an addict, so they stopped returning my telephone calls. I ended up doing things I'd rather forget."<br /><br />Chester paused for a sip of coffeeâ€¦ "One day, standing in front of a judge I was told I could take jail or A.A plus community service. So, I chose A.A. and service. I had nowhere to go, but a guy at one of the meetings told me about the Union Gospel Mission. So I stayed thereâ€¦ every night for months.â€<br /><br />â€œAfter a while I started to notice how badly the place needed new computers, but couldn't budget them in. I made a few calls to an old professor I knew at Caltech, and he sent me all the parts I needed to build new computers, custom made for the shelter. They had never seen so many computers in one place, and so high end. They were afraid they'd get stolen. I showed them how to lock them down, and use them. â€œ<br /><br />â€œEventually I migrated from resident to volunteer, and eventually honorary staff." &nbsp;I create code and do odd projects for money, and I make enough in my spare time to live better than most people, so I spend most of my time helping people down at the mission.â€<br /><br />â€œConsulting for the N.S.A. gave me a big break back into the world of coding professionally. I work for them, my record disappears (and so do any news articles about it). It's not the life my parents had in mindâ€¦ but it's more satisfying than making rich people a lot of money so that they can spend it on their mistresses and toys--"<br /><br />Chester paused and cocked his head, "What the?"<br /><br />Code was re-writing itself on his screen at lightening speed. The code kept re-writing, but a chat box appeared.<br /><br />"bds33s@ll: You were taking too long. Hope you don't mind."<br /><br />"Jazmin." Chester said.<br /><br />He typed back. "r@0stedch35tnts: What are you doing in my hack?"<br /><br />"B.D. What?" Eta asked.<br /><br />"Baby Doe Sees All. She was found crying in a dumpster after a botched late term abortion. The tag on her feet when Ms. Wu went to get her said "Baby Doe". Jazmin was the name Ms. Wu gave her, but the Baby Doe became something she latched on to."<br /><br />"That's so sad."<br /><br />"Sad, but too common. Planned Parenthood is nothing the media makes them out to be. They were founded by racists as a form of population control. Kill black babies, and they don't grow up to be black adults. That's why you see more Planned Parenthood clinics in minority neighborhoods than anywhere else. Something else people don't know is how many mothers either die, or are permanently injured at those clinics. If a few black mothers get hurt in the genocide, who's going to notice? That's their plan anyway."<br /><br />"How is that possible? How can people not know this?"<br /><br />"Politicians and Media are paid well to keep it out of the press. It's a low class slaughter house, pretending to be a 'women's health clinic'. Jazmin's arm was hurt when the doctor who barely made it through grad school botched the abortion. He did not even get a charge pressed against him. They are worse than Al Capone ever was. A few of us Hacktivists have started attacking their websites, shutting them down, publishing secret records, etc. We're starting to turn the tide, but only just barely."<br /><br />"bds33s@ll: I've been in your hack for hours, but you were boring me with the long hand. So I thought I'd help. But I want half the prize money this time."<br /><br />"r@0stedch35tnts: There is no prize money. This is a serious hack.â€<br /><br />"bds33s@ll: If you say so. I guess I still help, but prize money would be better.â€<br /><br />"r@0stedch35tnts: I suppose it could be done faster this way. Only this once."<br /><br />Pardon me a moment, this part will take some concentration working with two." Chester's' fingers where flying again.<br />It's amazing how fast the kids fingers could type. The girls were faster. The two of them ebbed and flowed through the code like a dance.<br /><br />This kid could type in his coding language, faster that Eta could think in English. Chester was something else. She called him â€œkidâ€, but he wasnâ€™t that much younger than her. Not bad on the eyes either.<br /><br />As he was distracted with code, she wandered the room studying his life. You can never turn off the solider, or the spy. Besides, even pointless analysis of the contents of his bookshelf was better than the thoughts of what might be happening to her mind. Insanity was creeping just outside the door. She could feel its cold presence. No. She's not allowing herself to think that way. She must stay focused.<br /><br />Even if that means distracting herself with "Quantum Mechanics for Mathematicians by Leon A. Takhtajan". Really? Who is this guy?! Eta looked out the window and watched some birds pecking in the yardâ€¦<br /><br />"Hey, this is interesting." Chester waived her over.<br /><br /><br />The Tower<br /><br /><br /><br />Hopefully the Intel Chester gave them was still current. Combined with his half real visit the other day, Ash was feeling confident at least. Chester can only hack computers, not notebooks. So if Ash's "trip" was accurate they needed that notebook.<br /><br />Ash told Eta and Chester that he had the surreal walk through, about the notebook, but he left out the part about his having a companion or guide. Visions are one thingâ€¦ Other worldly beings? That just seemedâ€¦ too weird to talk about just yet.â€<br /><br />They left Ash's Vulcan at Chester's place. Just in case someone spotted them, they could not risk having it tied back to Ash through registration tags. An Uber dropped them off at town nearby, and they worked their way through the sand and brush under cover of night to get to the Esion Eti'hw.<br /><br />The compound didn't seem heavily guarded, Ash knew that missing personnel would just trigger the alarm. He snuck in through a side window after the guards had passed. He could see men in lab coats running tests and reading outputs.<br /><br />There was the slight scent of rubbing alcohol, lemon scented.<br /><br />Ash opened the door of an unused room, and got out of the hallway, out of sight.<br /><br />Eta followed closely behind a few moments later.<br /><br />She looked tired. Sheâ€™s not sleeping well, he thought to himself.<br /><br />Moving their way across the room to another doorway on the opposite side of the large room, a slight opening allowed enough sight-line to assess the situation on the other side. They would need to be quick.<br /><br />Ash and Eta moved silently and swiftly down the hall toward the office towers.<br /><br />They were dark, so odds were that they were under light guard and they could be in and out without being seen.<br /><br />The elevator would be easier, but that would alert someone to its use. The stairs would be longer, but they would have a greater chance of avoiding their being spotted. Hopefully the cameras were already looped, they'd have to rely on Chester to complete that part of the mission. They didn't want to risk breaking radio silence to confirm. If he saw them heading into a trap, he'd break silence to alert them.<br /><br />As they entered the main floor of the office suite they checked infrared and found no motion detectors in the immediate area.<br /><br />Eta made quick work of the office doors, and they were both inside. It was just as he remembered in his... visit?<br /><br />They made their way to the back of the room, and Eta walked up to the windows. "So... You just stepped out. Huh?"<br /><br />"I'm not sure I'd do that again, under normal circumstances, but yep. Just walked right out."<br /><br />"That had to be a vision or dream right? It didn't really happen?"<br /><br />Ash felt along the edge for the depression the CEO had used earlier. "I don't know. It felt real, I wasn't sleeping before or after. What is a vision anyway? I'm no theologian, but I felt like I was really here."<br /><br />"Still, real or not, stepping out of a perfectly safe building... Feels scary from where I'm standing right now."<br /><br />"It was. Not gonna lie." Ash pressed the area, and the side popped out with a hiss." Found it.... Shoot!"<br /><br />"What?"<br /><br />"It's empty."<br /><br />"Well that sucks... Waste of a perfectly good trip."<br /><br />"We still have to get out. He probably takes it with him when he leaves, I wish I'd thought of it."<br /><br />Making their way out of the office, down the hall, and back down the stairs, footsteps and chatter below in the stairwell gave them pause. Ten levels down. The guards were making their rounds, chatting about the latest football game. Ash looked up, and didn't see anyone that direction. It would be a tight race, but it was the plan B they had discussed. They kept the foot falls as silent as possible as they made their way back up the stairs, trying to keep an ear on the guards below, and not alert them to their presence.<br /><br />A few floors up, and they heard the sound of radio chatter below.<br /><br />They stopped to listen.<br /><br />The guard room couldn't see the guards in the stair well.<br /><br />The loop had been discovered, they were compromised, but no-one knew they were here yet, they heard the guards coming up the stairs faster now.<br /><br />They couldn't risk breaking radio silence to discuss it with Chester, in fear that the guards below would hear.<br /><br />Ash and Eta kept to the walls and made their way quickly. If they stepped light enough the guards wouldn't hear them over their incessant chatter and yelling "Clear".<br /><br />Ash was so focused on the guards below, that he failed to watch the doorways as they passed them. The door opened just as they passed it, and lights flashed from the rifle.<br /><br />Or was that a rifle?<br /><br />It was a bright blue light... not the yellow red muzzle flash it should have been.<br /><br />Ash knew it as he was going down...<br /><br />...I hate tasers...<br /><br />In a one on one fight, Ash could have come back fighting, but a then second taser hit him. The guards from below had caught up.<br /><br />Three, no four men were on him, holding him down. The needle pinching his arm would mean powerful sleep.<br /><br />These were professionals. Now he knew what it felt like to be the victim of a smash and grab. Such a rookie mistake too, letting his guard down.<br /><br />They should have come in slower, or divided their attention.<br /><br />More surveillance.<br /><br />More reconnaissance.<br /><br />As he felt himself going under the power of sedation, he forced his eyes slightly open, he could see a figure coming toward him dressed in a black suit.<br /><br />He closed his eyes, but he heard a sigh.<br /><br />It sounded like disappointment.<br /><br />Was that himself sighing?<br /><br />Darkness came over him...<br /><br />***<br /><br />... The room always started out so bright. The white room. White walls, cold metal, hissing from overhead... the familiar dream, the dream he'd had every night for eight years now... The dark room would be next. He hated the dark room. But the white room wasn't so bad, except for that incessant hissing.<br /><br />***<br />The Escape<br /><br /><br /><br />Eta ran for all her legs would carry her, three guards were behind her.<br /><br />She was losing them, she could outrun these mall cops any day. Up ahead she saw the door open, and one came toward her.<br /><br />Running time was over for the moment. She'd have to stand her ground against this one, and the three behind.<br /><br />She saw the Taser flash, she side swiped and it made no contact.<br /><br />She used the momentum to grab the Taser and pull it toward her, while she swung her elbow into his chin.<br /><br />Whipping around she tossed him like a rag doll at the other three coming up the stairs.<br /><br />In another life, she could have just shot them all and been done with it, but these were innocent guards. She broke into their facility.<br /><br />These were not enemy combatants, and she couldnâ€™t just blow them all away.<br /><br />Still, getting caught isnâ€™t an option either.<br /><br />For split fractions of a second time slowed down. Should she stand against these four or had she thrown them off enough to regain her lead?<br /><br />Nope. One had gotten passed the others without losing momentum.<br /><br />He came at her with the Taser too.<br /><br />What is it with these guys and Tasers?<br /><br />She allowed the wires to fly passed as she used the stairwell handle for grip and plucked herself up into the air, and landed a kick right to the face.<br /><br />He hurled back into the other three who were just getting untangled.<br /><br />That was the break in momentum she needed.<br /><br />Eta flew up the stairs faster now, highest speed. She couldn't worry about Ash right now. He could handle himself. If he'd been caught, she couldn't help him if she were caught too.<br /><br />Up the stairs, out the access door to the roof. She pulled her side arm and fired three rounds into the padlock and landed a good jump kick into the door, which flew open before her.<br /><br />She tore a piece of her jacket and let it hang on a bolt near the top of the emergency exit ladder. She then threw the jacket down the side of the building. It caught the wind, and landed out toward the edge of the field. They would hunt her down in the fields, while she went another way.<br /><br />Meanwhile, she found an access panel, and hid inside the ducting.<br /><br />She waited for a half hour to ensure that they were all on the hunt for her elsewhere, and then Eta slowly exited the access panel.<br /><br />She worked her way down the fire escape on the dark side of the building.<br /><br />She could see they'd taken the ruse. Whole rounds of guards with flash lights were working their way through the field, others in vehicles were driving the field looking for farther signs of escape. There were even two helicopters searching the fields further out.<br /><br />Helicopters? Man, security is tighter here than we thought.<br /><br />Eta noticed that two of the guards on break had left their uniform jackets hanging on the stairwell when they ran out to join the chase.<br /><br />"Fortuitous.", she whispered.<br /><br />She donned the jacket, and now matched the other guards, the pants were so similar they'd never have been inspected, even close up.<br /><br />She took the tie back from her hair, and let it down with a shake. She walked in a hurried manner, but purposed to look like someone on a mission for the boss (not someone running for her life) toward the gate.<br /><br />She slipped out the back gate right toward all the fuss, and took a hummer with her.<br /><br />Why do they always leave the keys in these things?<br /><br />She picked up a guard along the way, and ordered him to watch from the passenger side and she looked out from the driverâ€™s side. As they whirred along, he tried to talk once, and she reprimanded him for not paying attention.<br /><br />"We have a fugitive on the loose and you want to make small talk soldier?"<br /><br />"No Ma'am!â€ came his reply. He didn't so much as look her direction for the rest of the journey.<br /><br />She stopped along the road.<br /><br />"Hey, let's split up. We can cover more ground. You check over that hill. If you see anything call it in. I'll head down the other way, and we'll meet back here in a half hour." She barked.<br /><br />He gave a nod, avoiding her stair directly, and ran off over the hill.<br /><br />Good puppy.<br /><br />She took off down the road and toward the town.<br /><br />Eta pulled in behind a truck stop, lost the flack jacket, and twisted the belly of her under shirt into a knot.<br /><br />Nothing would attract attention away from her face faster than this get up.<br /><br />Eta could not wait for an Uber at this time of night. She found an older Ford Escort parked on the darker side of the building, late 1990's model. She pulled out her Leatherman and worked on hot-wiring the ignition. In two minutes she was driving up the on ramp toward the I-10.<br /><br />She need to break radio silence, but not yet, not until she was sufficiently out of range.<br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />Traitor<br /><br /><br /><br />Dr.Briar put his face his hands and sighed. Ash was in his custody, but Eta was missing.<br /><br />How did they manage to find him so quickly? Normally, when a patient finds the site, they contact him directly. Maybe the attack in Oregon was backfiring on them. If they do not find her, there could be hell to pay.<br /><br />A blinking caught his attention. His monitor whirred to life, and the cursor was blinking on the screen.<br /><br />bds33s@ll: You said if I ever had any information for you, I should contact you. How much is that worth? He recognized the handle. One of the orphanage kids that he found when their former IT man was working for him. She used to come and assist from time to time, some kind of mentorship program. Unlike the IT man, this kid was willing to hack anything for a price.<br /><br />Dr.Briar: It depends on the Intel. What do you have?<br /><br />bds33s@ll: My former mentor is using my code to hack your system. I can lock him out. Whatâ€™s that worth to you?<br /><br />Dr.Briar: $50,000 would be enough?<br /><br />bds33s@ll: Make it $100,000.<br /><br />Dr.Briar: How about $75,000, itâ€™s not worth any more than than.<br /><br />bds33s@ll: Deal. Youâ€™ll have your system up and running in 45 minutes or Iâ€™ll give you a $10,000 discount if it takes longer.<br /><br />Dr.Briar: Agreed.<br /><br />***<br /><br />Trapped<br /><br /><br /><br />Ash was on an island in the South Pacific. He'd just sent a UAV with "stolen" technology back to base. Routine training mission.<br /><br />There was a flash of light... the light was gone and it was from a pen light.<br /><br />A man in a white coat stood before him, he couldn't make out his face... The scene went black again.<br /><br /><br /><br />***<br /><br />The silence was deafening. Except for that constant hiss from above.<br /><br />The light was still too bright to open his eyes. But the sound was familiar.<br /><br />I know this place...<br /><br />That stupid dream again.<br /><br />He could feel cold steel against his wrists.<br /><br />This was new. He didn't recall being locked to a chair in his dream.<br /><br />Then he heard... click. click. click.<br /><br />A clock?<br /><br />Ash forced his eyes open, squinting at first, to see a room very similar to one from his dream.<br /><br />As his eyes focused, he could see that this one was longer, the table was at one end. And there was a single clock on the wall. This room had a pronounced and obvious door, with a glass window.<br /><br />"Oh good... you are awake. I'll be with you in just a little while."<br /><br />That voice. I know that voice.<br /><br />Ash took in his surroundings. Nothing much to see. Nothing but white walls, white chair, white table, white clock. Silver Handcuffs on his wrists.<br /><br />No silly outfit, he was simply wearing his black gear from the night before, minus the weapons. Blood had dried on his sleeve where the guard got a good kick with his boot. It looked like they patched him up, maybe a few stitches in the shoulder.<br /><br />Ash noticed four cameras, one in each corner.<br /><br />The door opened, and in walked a small bald man in a lab coat.<br /><br />"Dr. Briar."<br /><br />"Good morning Col. Ashland. It's nice to have you back among us. You took a pretty nasty fall last night, and put three of my guards in the hospital."<br /><br />"Glad I could help." Ash Smiled.<br /><br />Always make sure your captor knows you are in charge.<br /><br />Dr. Briar returned the smile. "It seems you and I have some unfinished business. As soon as we track down your friend, we'll have questions for her too. My best head hunters are looking for her now.â€<br /><br />He placed a file on the desk. â€œTell me, Col. Ashland, how much do you remember about me?"<br /><br />"You are Dr. Briar, we met outside the cafe in Oregon, and you smell like peppermint candy."<br /><br />"Yes, all true. But how about before that time at the coffee shop, is there anything else you remember about me?"<br /><br />"No. Just what Iâ€™ve read about you online. Should there be?"<br /><br />"We shall see." Dr Briar pulled out some wires and pads. "While you were out, you were injected with a serum of my own design. It will help you be more forth coming. I'm simply going to attach these to you for monitoring. I promise it won't hurt. We tried putting them on while you were out, but you kept sweating them off...." He placed the pads on Ash's temples, neck, chest, and forearms.<br /><br />"Tell me, Col. Ashland, how long have you been having these nightmares?"<br /><br />"Are you my psychologist now?<br /><br />"Let's just say that I am an interested party."<br /><br />"I get bad dreams, left over from the war. Not that it's any of your business."<br /><br />Dr. Briar continued to press with question after pointless question.<br /><br />Ash found it to be the oddest interrogation he has ever been aware of. Not once did Dr. Briar ask him why he broke in, or what he hoped to accomplish, or what he had done while in the facility.<br /><br />He just kept pressing him with questions about his life, habits, and preferences in food. If he was trying to bore him into slipping up and giving an important detail, it just might work.<br /><br />It was the kind of small talk Ash always tried to avoid in normal life, and yet he was tied to a chair. So he could either answer these pointless questions, or say nothing.<br /><br />He was pretty sure that nothing was a bad option, because they'd probably put him back away for a while. He would use this opportunity to counter-interrogate. So he kept Dr. Briar talking while he answered the Life Survey.<br /><br />After what felt like hours of incessant questioning, but the clock assured him it was only 45 minutes, Dr. Briar placed is pen on top of his notebook, and leaned back into the chair. His eyebrows furrowed, and the skin around his fat nose wrinkled.<br /><br />Ash, sat back in his chair, and let the urge to break his own thumbs to escape the handcuffs pass for a moment.<br /><br />The door was still locked, and he didn't want to risk killing the good Doctor. He was most definitely someone who could not be trusted, but he wasn't yet sure he needed to die.<br /><br />Not yet anyway.<br /><br />A bright flash, Ash closed his eyes.<br /><br />When his vision cleared, the Doctor was still sitting, staring at his notebook.<br /><br />Bob was now standing behind him, smiling again. He walked over to the desk, and leaned down, staring up at Dr. Briar. His smile faded slightly, and he shook his head. Bob stood again, and nodded to Ash.<br /><br />Without any audible words, Ash felt him say "Play along".<br /><br />Bob put his finger on the notebook that held the good doctors attention, and pulled it ever so slightly across the desk toward Ash.<br /><br />Dr. Briar sat back abruptly, eyes wide, and gasped.<br /><br />Bob again pulled the notebook closer to Ash.<br /><br />"What are you reading there Doc?" He decided to play along.<br /><br />"How-?" Dr. Briar cut himself off. "How are you doing this?"<br /><br />"I'm not doing anything." Ash didn't want to lie, per say.<br /><br />"What were you thinking just now, while I was reading?"<br /><br />"I was just bored of the silence, and wondering what you were reading... Hence my question."<br /><br />"But how did you move the notebook?"<br /><br />"Move the notebook?" Ash raised his voice in fained shock at the suggestion. "I did no such thing. I'm sitting over here. Are you feeling alright?"<br /><br />The good doctor stood up, and stood next to Ash. "I want you to think about that notebook, just like you were a moment ago. Think with all your mind how you want to read it."<br /><br />"OK, whatever you say doc." Ash squinted at the notebook, fighting back a grin.<br /><br />Bob nudged the book half way across the desk.<br /><br />"My goodness... keep going".<br /><br />Ash kept squinting, Bob picked the notebook off the desk, floated it into the air, the slowly carried it over to Ash. Inch by inch, he brought it over.<br /><br />Ash felt this was the stupidest parlor trick he'd ever seen, but then if he could not see Bob, it would be quite a different show.<br /><br />Dr. Briar took a step back, behind Ash, holding his breath.<br /><br />Bob placed the notebook gently on Ash's lap.<br /><br />"Astonishing-" Dr. Briar let out. "I must get the readings. I have to go... Uh... Don't go anywhere."<br /><br />"I'm handcuffed to a chair, where am I going?"<br /><br />"Yes. Right." Dr. Briar grabbed the notebook and half walked, half tripped over his own feet as he ran out of the door, leaving Ash alone in the rectangular white room.<br /><br />Bob looked very pleased with himself.<br /><br />Ash let out a little laugh.<br /><br /><br /><br />Change of plans<br /><br /><br /><br />Dr. Briar sits at the monitor, reviewing the footage, watching as they place Ash in the chair, and cuff him in place. Similar setting to when he first met him.<br /><br />***<br /><br />Thaddeus, we cannot push him any further, he's already been in the room longer than most others, and his vitals are dangerously out of control. We must put an end to this testâ€¦ or give him a rest and start again.<br /><br />"You Dr. Briar, like these experiments, are temporary and expendableâ€¦ &nbsp;you would do well to remember that."<br /><br />A chill crept over his spine, from his tail bone, up his neck, and into his head.. His skin became cold as the realization dawned on himâ€¦ He was never the one in control. When he'd brought Thaddeus on, he'd been so hungry for the financial backing and the seeming eagerness that he showed in the project, he'd never stopped to consider that Thaddeus would take over.<br /><br />"â€¦I'll be in the observation lounge that I paid so much money to build. The Air Force needs results. If we lose him, I will handle it. Proceed." Thaddeus stepped out of the room, and walked down the hall.<br /><br />Dr. Briar slumped into his chair with the realizationâ€¦ he'd sold his science, his soul, to this partnership. Without this financial backing, he'd have to pull out and stop everything. Shameful though it may be, the scientist in him was curious to see what would happen if he pushed the patient over the edge.<br /><br />So he turned back to the monitor, and kept one eye on the vitals, then turned pushed the slider one notch higher. "It's beginning... the biometrics just spiked."<br /><br />"Good." The answer was curt.<br /><br />The previous test subjects had nowhere near the discipline that Ash did. This should be an interesting observation. He'd been incredible so far. Getting a trained military man, accustomed to being alone for days and weeks, a survival expert. He had already outlasted every scientist, street volunteer, and audio geek they had ran through the tests. Many of them didn't make it through the prep room, but they never had anyone last this long, he'd been in there for three days and now he's starting to show signs of a dimensional tear.<br /><br />"You are a genius!", Dr. Briar spoke into the observation lounge speaker.<br /><br />"Yes, Dr. Briar I am! That is why you brought me here."<br />&nbsp;Thaddeus replied. "To fix this reject project and bring something valuable out of it. Keep me appraised of any changes, I'm leaving for now."<br /><br />Dr. Briar could see through the window as the black suited man turned and left the room. His tuft of white hair bouncing along as he walked away.<br /><br />***<br /><br />That was 8 years ago, the beginning of the end of his life.<br /><br />Now he was still here, cleaning up the mess his Benefactor had left him with. Dr. Briar watched the vital signs on the monitor, much more advanced than the ones they used on Ash's first visits.<br /><br />They now used a bio implant chip. It could be placed as a sub-dermal, nothing more than an insect bite to the unaware patient.<br /><br />Thanks to the fight last night, they were able to put one in Ash's arm, disguised in the patch up they did of the existing cut.<br /><br />Hopefully, he'd never know it was there.<br /><br />He slowly pushed up the fader labeled, "White Noise".<br /><br />It was a bit of a misnomer, as white noise is an even amount of each frequency, and this was adjusted to their purposes, but it came through as a hissing sound nevertheless.<br /><br />Ash was stirring, he'd be awake soon. He would go and complete the mission. His stomach turned at the thought.<br /><br />The phone chirped, and he picked it up. "Dr. Briar speaking."<br /><br />"It's Thaddeus. Are you positive you cannot find the Problem Child without him?"<br /><br />"Yes, I'm sure. We've tried everything, he's evaded us at every turn. We need EH's to find an EH. Col. Ashland has shown exceptional promise, the most impressive we've seen."<br /><br />"Very well, he may still be of some use to us. He will need..." Thaddeus paused. "... Motivation."<br /><br />Dr. Briar's stomach tightened.<br /><br /><br />Regroup<br /><br /><br /><br />Two hours passed since she had left the Tower.<br /><br />Eta was feeling the intense pressure to get back to Chester and make a game plan. Her training told her to make sure she wouldn't be followed first. She'd borrowed a car from the Denny's parking lot. She'd driven in the wrong directions for several miles, making frequent turns, watching for familiar faces or vehicles... after an hour she was sure she wasn't followed, or found.<br /><br />She parked the car at a different Denny's, placed the keys into an envelope with some cash (enough to buy a better car), and put it on the floor board by the pedals. She called an anonymous tip from a pay phone.<br /><br />With luck, the Police would have the car before the owner knew it was missing. She'd wiped it down for prints and evidence.<br /><br />Five minutes later, the Metro Bus took her to the Metro Rail.<br /><br />That was four hours ago, when she arrived at Union Station. She'd walked from there. The car they had rented was in a safe place, it was rented under an assumed name, so the Institute shouldn't be able to find it. They'd have to go back for it when it was safe, unless Ash could get to it without being seen.<br /><br />When she walked up to Chesterâ€™s place and stepped in, she could hear him typing away.<br /><br />He turned around in relief. "Oh man! I'm so glad you are safe!"<br /><br />"I am, but I think they have Ash. I came back here to re-group and re-gear, then I'm headed back up there."<br /><br />"Actually, Ash wasn't the one I was worried about. You were the one I was worried about..."<br /><br />He paused. His eyes were not on her face. â€œUm. Nice outfit.â€<br /><br />Eta felt the skepticism rise up, she could always feel it in her left eyebrow. Heat run up her neck.<br /><br />â€œHow did you get here?â€<br /><br />â€œThe bus.â€<br /><br />"Good choice.â€ Chester had turned back to the computer. â€œIt worked. I've tapped into every system they have, and they don't know I'm inside. They have him in a room controlled by electronic locks. These people obviously never considered a real cyber threat after I left. All my back doors are still in place."<br /><br />The monitor showed Ash strapped to an expensive looking chair, in the middle of a very white room. His arms restrained with metal cuffs.<br /><br />"That room feels familiar." She said, mostly to herself.<br /><br />"Now, I can work miracles with systems, but there is nothing I can do about those cuffs." Chester threw up his hands. "That is all up to--"<br /><br />â€œWhat is it?â€<br /><br />â€œSomeone is re-writing my code.â€<br /><br />â€œIs that possible?â€<br /><br />â€œAnything is possible, but how did they find me in the system so quickly?â€<br /><br />The screen went black. A single message â€œbds33s@ll: Your session has been terminated.â€<br /><br />â€œNoâ€¦ she thinks this is a game. She has no idea whatâ€™s she just did!â€ Chester put his hands up in the air, stood and started pacing.<br /><br />His face and neck became red, he pick up his laptop and tossed it against the wall. Shattering it into little pieces.<br /><br />â€œShe put a virus into my system while I wasnâ€™t looking. That PC is dead. I have to go upstairs to Big Bertha.â€<br /><br />â€œBig Bertha?â€ Eta said, â€œReally?â€<br /><br />Chester smiled again, slightly, and shrugged. â€œItâ€™s a monster machine. I need to hurry. I have no idea whatâ€™s going on in there right now.â€<br /><br />"You monitor from here. I'll stand by in case he needs a ride."<br /><br />"Where will you get another ride?" Chester glanced back. â€œYou took the last bus to get here, no more lines running tonight.â€<br /><br />"I'll improvise." Eta held up the new burner phones Chester had prepared just in case, and waved it at him. Then turned around and headed out the door.<br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />Olive Branch<br /><br /><br /><br />The Doctor ran out of the room, very excited. That was an hour ago.<br /><br />Click<br /><br />Click<br /><br />Click<br /><br />The clock beat away on the wall.<br /><br />Bob sat on the desk, swinging his legs back and forth, looking very pleased with himself.<br /><br />Ash still wasn't sure what to think of this. The Tower checked out almost as expected, minus the missing journal. But, why lead him here, just to get caught with no journal? So this wasn't an hallucination, right? If this wasn't, what do you call it?<br /><br />The door opened, the doctor came back in, but this time he was accompanied by the same white haired man<br />Ash had seen in the office.<br /><br />The white tuft of bouncing hair walked over and sat on the edge of the desk, staring at Ash.<br /><br />"Show me" Thaddeus opened his hand, and there was a pen laying on his palm.<br /><br />"I'm not sure it works like that." Ash said "But I'll try.<br /><br />He gave his best look of stern concentration. He tried to pretend it was a Cheeseburger. This was not because he believed that he was actually doing anything, but it helped his performance. He was really hungry, and a Cheeseburger sounded great right now... Or Thai. Yes. Thai...<br /><br />Bob walked over to the outstretched hand, and pick up the pen, gently at first. He floated the pen around the the space between them. Up. Down. Making figure signs in the air. Then with a grin, Bob tossed it up in the air, and it stuck into a sound proof ceiling panel, like an arrow in the center of its bullseye.<br /><br />"Sorry about" Ash said, fighting back a smile."I think I lost control for a moment there."<br /><br />"Interesting." Thaddeus said, turning to the doctor. "You are sure about this?"<br /><br />The doctor shrugged but said nothing.<br /><br />"Very well." Thaddeus pulled a chair around, and sat face to face with Ash. "I have just decided, against my better judgment, to trust you Col Ashland."<br /><br />"OK. Thank you... I think. Trust me how?"<br /><br />Offer trust to the captive, good technique. Ash used it himself in Afghanistan.<br /><br />"Your friends have attempted a hack of our security system which means that the system is now resetting. You should know that the reset process has also given us the ability to back trace them. I'm not sure how you managed to hire our former IT man, but this should prove quite useful for us. And dangerous for you."<br /><br />Ash decided to go along with whatever this was for the time being. "You have my attention."<br /><br />"I had determined to have you killed today, when I got everything I needed from you." He leaned back a little.<br />"Just like I was supposed to have you killed in Oregon. Apparently I needed to hire better men."<br /><br />"You hired Russians..." Ash snorted. "I could have heard them from a mile away. Next time hire an American, or at least Australian."<br /><br />"I will take note of that, thank you." He did not smile. "Chester Jandihar is our former hire. We have all his records, including his home address. I'm assuming this is where I can pick up Eta too. I could have the three of you removed from the playing field . Quietly. Then I could move on to other, more pressing projects."<br /><br />Ash felt his eye brows crunch. He could break his own thumbs to get out of the cuffs, but then he'd be inhibited from a real fight. And this guy looked like he could handle himself.<br /><br />"Instead..." Thaddeus continued, "I will offer you an olive branch, of sorts."<br /><br />"I'm listening."<br /><br />"I want to put my hit for the three of you on hold."<br /><br />"The three of us?" Ash sat a little straighter.<br /><br />"You brought poor Chester into this, he must be accounted for in the equation." Thaddeus took a deep breath. "As much as I do not like loose ends, I have a problem that is worse than you. We call him The Problem Child. I need you to find him, and bring him to me. Alive. If you can do that, I will cancel my hit on all three of you. If you fail, the hit will proceed. Do you understand?"<br /><br />"Yes."<br /><br />"Do you accept my offer?"<br /><br />â€œHow do I know you wonâ€™t carry out your hit when weâ€™re done?â€<br /><br />â€œYou donâ€™t.â€<br /><br />Ash studied his face, he was serious. "How much time do we have to find this Problem Child?"<br /><br />Thaddeus glanced to his wrist, looking at a very expensive analogue watch. "3 weeks. If you haven't found him by then, you are of no use to me."<br /><br />"Agreed." Ash sighed. What have I myself got into now?<br /><br />"The doctor here will fill you in. Long stories bother me." Thaddeus stood, and brushed his pant legs, and tugged at his jacket. Then left the room.<br /><br /><br />Ultimatum<br /><br /><br /><br />The doctor looked distraught, sitting behind his desk like a broken puppy. "That's my benefactor... Nice guy huh?"<br /><br />"So Doctor." Ash said. "Looks like I have a mission assignment. Care to fill me in on what I need to know?"<br /><br />"Tell me-" Dr. Briar stood and began pacing back and forth behind the desk. â€œAre you familiar with Psychotropic Warfare?"<br /><br />"Sure" Ash replied. "We used various forms in the field, for crowd dispersement."<br /><br />"Well you only experienced the tip of the iceberg. Those were the weapons that were tested and released. Many thousands of others are being researched by DARPA, and those funded by people like them."<br /><br />"Allow me to indulge in a little back ground, this is my life's work I'm about to share with you." The doctor slowed his pacing. "I am only authorized to tell you what you need to know for this mission, but you will need context so bear with me.â€<br /><br />â€œUnderstood.â€<br /><br />â€œAt the turn of the century one of the most brilliant men of his time, Nicola Tesla, performed experiments with electricity. Some mathematicians and scientists believe that Tesla's work would provide the basis for understanding seemingly paranormal phenomena like telepathy."<br /><br />The doctor stopped pacing, and motioned to Ash. "Have you heard of Extremely Low Frequency, or ELF?"<br /><br />"You got me there," Ash sat back again, "No idea".<br /><br />"When researchers started applying Tesla's equations in electrical waves to sound waves, they found interesting applications. The human ear can generally hear between 20 Hertz to 20,000 Hertz. Exceptions outside that range are possible, but rare. ELF started as experiments using "sound" frequencies below the level of human hearing. Think of a dog hearing a dog whistle, but you can't. Same idea, but the dog whistle is higher than normal human hearing, and the ELF's are lower. When ELF's are produced at high pressure levels, think volume, they have been found to cause interesting physiological results."<br /><br />"Dr. Briar, your enthusiasm is hard to miss, but I'm missing the point I think."<br /><br />"Yes, yes, bear with me and I will get to the point, I promise. Research has shown that external ELF magnetic fields induce electric fields and currents in the body which, at very high field strengths, cause nerve and muscle stimulation and changes in nerve cell excitability in the central nervous systemâ€¦â€<br /><br />Dr. Briar turned, Ash thought he had the look of a giddy school child showing off his new toy.<br /><br />â€œâ€¦but they can do more than just irritate the body and mind. Prolonged exposure to ELF's from normal electronic appliances, like TV's and Smart Phones, have been shown to activate the ATM-Chk2-p21 pathway in HaCaT cells, inhibiting cell proliferation."<br /><br />Ash raised an eyebrow, â€œMeaning?â€<br /><br />"Meaning, that they change the brain and cell structure itself!" The doctor's eyes almost glowed at the suggestion. "Several decades ago I was working as a Higher Research Scientist at the National Physical Laboratory in England. I was in charge of a project to study Optimum Biological Frequency Resonance, or OBFR. The hypothesis was that our biological system is "tuned into" the background frequency of our planet, which is a steady pulse of 7.83 hertz. While testing several new acoustic chambers I stumbled across an interesting development in our Negative Sound chamber--"<br /><br />Ash interrupted, "I'm sorry, Negative Sound?"<br /><br />"Yes, it does 'sound' odd, doesn't it?â€ the doctor chuckled at his own joke. â€œThe room is located 100's of feet below ground level to avoid any level of ambient noise, and then covered in deep sound baffling wall to wall, floor to ceiling. The subject is asked to sit in a chair in the center of the room. Because any sound you make does not bounce back to you from the wall, as it would in any normal space we live in, the result can feel as though the sound were negative. This simply means that the subject is experiencing less sound than they have ever experienced, not that the sound is actually below zero, that is impossible of course."<br /><br />Ash could feel his gut sink... "Are you telling me that room actually exists?"<br /><br />The Doctor paused, "Yes, why?"<br /><br />"It's from my nightmares, I see that room every night."<br /><br />"Well, that is to be expected. You see, you were one of many volunteers who were subjected to our experiments during Operation White Noise."<br /><br />"Excuse me? Volunteers? I do not recall Volunteering for anything."<br /><br />"That is unfortunate. I will come to that, but please, allow me a moment to get to that."<br /><br />The Doctor took a sip of water, and sat down on the edge of the desk in front of Ash. "When we subjected our volunteers to the room, we noticed that some began to hallucinate. Most chalked that up to the vast capacity of the human mind to fill the void with something, anything, other than nothing.â€<br /><br />â€œMakes sense--â€<br /><br />â€œHowever, I had already begun separate experiments into paranormal research and ELF effects. We found that we could place the subject in a clean room first, and bombard them with white noise, which sounds like static. Call it an acoustic palate cleanser before the main meal. Slowly, over a period of time determined by the subjectâ€™s readings, we would introduce specific ELF's to determine their effect. We would then move them to the Negative Sound room, and continue only the ELF's from there. They could not hear the ELF's, but they were there being played in â€œsilenceâ€. At that point something amazing happened. The subjects were able to temporarily tear back the veil, and see into the beyond."<br /><br />"The beyond?" Ash could feel his jaw tense, "What do you mean, beyond?"<br /><br />"Subjects exposed to the 19 hertz would experience the 'haunted house' effect; skin crawling, feeling like someone passed them. At 6.8 hertz the subject would actually see dark creatures around them, and in some extreme cases hear them. Others were exposed to the 7.83 hertz range, and they would see light creatures.â€<br /><br />â€œSome, you for example,â€ pointing to Ash, â€œwere exposed to all three stages several times. Curiously, the exposure was not the only factor. Some subjects responded differently than others."<br /><br />"OK, I'm hearing you... But none of that explains why your 'benefactor' wants me dead, or what that has to do with your Problem Child."<br /><br />"Well... The experiments went fine. The test subjects did not seem to have any long term affects and were not able to duplicate the experiences outside the chamber. The only lasting affect was that prolonged exposure seemed to have an effect on short term memory. We believe this may have something to do with a symptom we have called the Eternity Effect."<br /><br />"Eternity Effect?"<br /><br />"The subject loses all sense of time and place, and has to be brought back down to reality before release. This often takes a few more weeks in the clean room. After that they were released, and no worse for the wear, except they have little to no memory of the experiment. That turned out good for us.â€ The Doctor hesitated. â€œUntil..."<br /><br />"Until... What?" Ash prodded.<br /><br />"We could not have known, never have expected..." The Doctor mumbled to himself.<br /><br />"Doctor Briar. What? Until What?"<br /><br />"Years later, as subjects came in contact with the naturally occurring ELF's, their symptoms reappeared outside the room, out in real life."<br /><br />Ash felt his mind cramp as the impact of those words dawned on him.<br /><br />"The old man, the crazy one you confronted, he was one of ours too. But he was a volunteer years before you were. The first time we realized this new result was when we were contacted by an emergency room. We were listed as the primary care physician in those days. The man had quit his job in the middle of the shift, and walked out into oncoming traffic on the freeway. When he was asked why, he was told the voices told him to. He was hurt, but they stabilized him. The next day he snuck out of his room, walked into traffic again, and died on impact. The resulting accident caused more fatalities."<br /><br />"That's terrible..."<br /><br />"We thought he was just a bad egg, predisposed to craziness before we got to him. But then it started happening again, and again. Not all of them, mind you, but more than could be statistically explained by predisposition.â€<br /><br />â€œThat sounds like a big problem, with legal and financial fallout sure to follow.â€<br /><br />Dr. Briar nodded. â€œWe now believe it has to do with the increase of technology everywhere. There are more ELF's and they build in standing waves in peopleâ€™s homes, jobs, cars, radio towers, cell towers, the list goes on. It was determined that we were responsible, and we needed to conduct damage control, before anyone outside could be become aware of usâ€¦had to prevent those problems you so aptly mentioned. But that was only part of the problem. We could not loose all the work we had done already, we were too invested.â€<br /><br />He stood again. â€œWe had to monitor each subject. Anyone showing symptoms would be put down before they could harm others."<br /><br />"You mean killed. I imagine that you know that is illegal?"<br /><br />"Of course, but the tests themselves were cutting edge, and corners were cut."<br /><br />â€œSo the tests were illegal too.â€<br /><br />â€œIn a manner of speaking. Off the books, certain agencies found the data useful, and looked the other way.â€ He sighed. â€œI only learned later that the funding for the project was not entirely above board. We could not tell the authorities about all the subjects. The lawsuits would cripple us. We decided that anyone showing symptoms would be locked away if we could arrange it, but that too risked exposure. Eventually we started putting them down. I only agreed in the most extreme of cases at first, but then once I was in, I could not get out. I could both continue my work and get my hands dirty, or I would be put down too." The doctor turned white at the thought.<br /><br />Ash let out a sigh, and wiped his face on his shoulder.<br /><br />"Which brings me to your task, Problem Child"<br /><br />"OK."<br /><br />"We know very little about him. His name was Walter Elias Vanderbilt, but that turned out to be an alias. In fact, his entire history, education, family back ground, it was all fabricated. Everything I know about him is on this Thumb Drive." The Doctor placed it in Ash's pocket.<br /><br />"Despite my best resources, I cannot find him. It's possible that you, with your Special Forces background, could think of something. Combined with your developing telekinetic abilities, and likely other abilities you havenâ€™t manifested yet, you may be a match for him."<br /><br />"Alright, let's just say that I'm buying your story for now. Something still doesn't add up. I was not released from some clean room. I went missing from a top secret training facility, and weeks later I was found on a desert island with no memory. How does that fit in to your volunteer story?"<br /><br />"You were special, you and your friend. We needed subjects from specific backgrounds that were not as likely to volunteer. So, we arranged to have them delivered. I cannot tell you more, I wish I could. I was the Chief Scientist, but I did not handle the... Business End. You showed up and I put you into the clean room before you woke. Afterward, you were delivered back to your people. I cannot tell you who, or how...â€ Doctor Briar leaned closer, "But I can tell you that we could not have done it without help from your own government. You be careful who you trust. I know DARPA funds were siphoned more than once for our project."<br /><br />Ash sat, silently. Staring Dr. Briar in the eye. Heâ€™s not lying.<br /><br />The Doctor walked around to his desk, and pulled open the drawer. He came back around, and dropped a second small white envelope into Ash's inside jacket pocket, then put his finger to his mouth.<br /><br />"I don't believe there is anything else I can tell you at the moment."<br /><br />He pressed a button on the wall behind him.<br /><br />â€œYes Doctor Briar." &nbsp;A man answered.<br /><br />"We are done in here." Dr. Briar walked to his desk, and stood to the side.<br /><br />Two guards entered the room, asked him to stand up. One had a bruise on his jaw, "Did I do that do you?"<br /><br />The guard did not smile. He un-cuffed Ash, and motioned to the door<br /><br /><br /><br />Recovered<br /><br /><br />Eta got the call from Chester. He managed to re-enter the hack and get camera feeds. Ash had been released and walked out of the back entrance. He left on foot, headed toward the beach.<br /><br />Eta drove the newly borrowed Corvette down to the coast. She saw a bum sitting on the bench with what appeared to be a newly acquired military grade jacket. She pulled up to him, and asked where he got it. Then he pointed her toward the beach shop nearby.<br /><br />"Thanks buddy." Eta nodded and pulled up along the curb in front of the shop.<br /><br />"Took you long enough." A voice spoke from behind her. She could feel her heart jump into her throat. How does he do that?<br /><br />"You could get shot..." She said without turning around. "... for sneaking up on a girl like that."<br /><br />"I could" Ash came up and leaned on the window of the convertible sheâ€™d â€˜borrowedâ€™, "but life is worth the risk some times."<br /><br />"So..." Eta pulled her sun glasses down. He was wearing the same pants from the night before, but he had torn them into cut-off shorts and he managed to acquire a new a new tank top that said â€œMalibu Styleâ€.<br />He had put on a few pounds since they worked together in Israel, but he didn't age badly. She cast the thought aside. No. Not him. "Now what grandpa?"<br /><br />"Now, we make sure I wasn't followed. Then I take these" Ash pulled two thumb drives out of a white envelope. "to Chester." He slipped them back into the envelope, then his pocket.<br /><br />â€œWe had visual, but no audio. Whatâ€™s on it? And why did he give it to you?â€<br /><br />â€œThe only thing in here was the thumb drive, and a name on the outside. â€˜Darkmatter.â€™ Weâ€™ll need Chester to debug it first."<br /><br />â€œThatâ€™s an interesting turn of events.â€ Eta said. â€œWhy, exactly, did he let you go?â€<br /><br />â€œItâ€™s a long story, Iâ€™m not sure that I trust himâ€¦ tell you on the way.â€ Ash jumped over the door into the passenger seat. â€œUhâ€¦ that is as soon as you tell me where you got a bright blue convertible 2016 Corvette Stingray.â€<br />Eta could feel the grin crawl across her face. She couldnâ€™t help it.<br /><br />&nbsp;â€œA bit conspicuousâ€¦ donâ€™t you think?â€ Ash turned back to Eta. He had his superior officer face on, but she could see the smile he was fighting back.<br /><br />â€œWillow is amazing for finding wealthy people selling their third homes in the recession. This guy still had his car collection in the garage in one of the photos, and his â€˜sophisticatedâ€™ key box wasnâ€™t that sophisticated.â€ She shrugged. â€œI had to do something with my time while I was waiting on Chester to tell me where to go.â€<br /><br />&nbsp;â€œHeyâ€ Ash interrupted her moment. Trade spots with me â€œIâ€™m driving.â€<br /><br />â€œHA!â€ Eta snorted. â€œGo stealâ€¦ erâ€¦ borrowâ€¦ your own sports car. Iâ€™m driving.â€ She looked him in the eye.<br />â€œBesides, we both know that Iâ€™m a better driver, and Iâ€™d like to return this in one piece so he never knows it was missing.â€<br /><br />Ash tilted his head and looked like he wanted to say something, then threw a glance at his shoulder stitches and put on his seat belt.<br /><br />Her fingers fumbled for the phone in her Cowboy Holster bag. She paid UrbanTool $115.00 for this glorified fanny pack. But she hated purses, and this was just that cool. She handed a phone to Ash, â€œThis one is yours, call Chester and let him know I found you.â€<br /><br />Then she pressed the start button and the engine roared to life.<br /><br /><br /><br />Observation<br /><br /><br /><br />Dr. Briar pulled up his monitor at his desk in the tower.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Status Report?<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Wild-card in play.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Good. Keep watch and monitor results.<br /><br />He pulled out up the footage of Chester's house. They had set up shop in the house across the street. One great thing about this recession, so many empty houses. The heat signatures showed three inside. Now they would lead him to the real prize.<br /><br />He pulled out his phone and sent a text to his new assistants.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Keep the ELF's playing at a constant 78 Dbl, aimed directly at the house. Report anything... Odd.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Roger<br /><br />The Dr. Scrolls to the list. Sends a text to another assistant.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Deliver the young orphan her prize.<br /><br />â—<span class="Apple-tab-span" style="white-space: pre;"> </span>Delivering now.<br />Memories<br /><br /><br />Ash back in his room, pondered the next move.<br /><br />Who is this Problem Child, and how do we even start to find him?<br /><br />He laid back on the bed, and closed his eyes.<br /><br />***<br /><br />He's back on the Island.<br /><br />A man in a lab coat, a balding man. It's Dr. Briar.<br /><br />He can see that now. "You should know, that these experiments have had wildly different results. You could be a Super Soldier, or a Vegetable, or nothing may happen at all. Are you sure you're ready for this?"<br /><br />"I am." Ash turned to a small changing room, where he dressed in a white bike suit.<br /><br />As he turned to the glass before entering the small white room, he looked back. Standing next to Dr. Briar was a manâ€¦ he couldnâ€™t see him, but he had a uniform.<br /><br />***<br /><br />Ash sat up in the bed.<br /><br />Sweat soaked his shirt.<br /><br />â€œI did this to myself.â€<br /><br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq" style="background-color: white; color: #333333; font-family: Verdana, Geneva, sans-serif; font-size: 15.4px; line-height: 19.6px;"><div><br /></div></blockquote><h4 style="background-color: white; color: #333333; font-family: Verdana, Geneva, sans-serif; font-size: 15.4px; line-height: 19.6px; margin: 0px; position: relative;">By&nbsp;<a href="https://plus.google.com/+DarrellWolfe/posts" style="color: #149adf; text-decoration: none;" target="_blank">Darrell Wolfe</a></h4><blockquote class="tr_bq" style="background-color: white; color: #333333; font-family: Verdana, Geneva, sans-serif; font-size: 15.4px; line-height: 19.6px;"><i>Storyteller | Creative | <a href="http://www.16personalities.com/infj-personality" style="color: #149adf; text-decoration: none;" target="_blank">INFJ</a>&nbsp;|&nbsp;<a href="https://www.gallupstrengthscenter.com/Purchase/en-US/Product?Path=Clifton%20StrengthsFinder" style="color: #149adf; text-decoration: none;" target="_blank">Intellection</a>&nbsp;|&nbsp;Ideation | Input | Learner | Achiever | Multipotentialite</i></blockquote><ul><li><i>See my full about page and disclosures: <a href="http://authorfun.blogspot.com/p/what-is-this.html" target="_blank">Click Here</a>.&nbsp;</i></li><li><i>Also check out my <a href="http://authorfun.blogspot.com/p/affiliate-store.html" target="_blank">Resources and Suggestions </a>for writers (<a href="http://authorfun.blogspot.com/p/affiliate-store.html" target="_blank">here</a>).&nbsp;</i></li><li><i>To Subscribe to the <a href="http://feeds.feedburner.com/AuthorFun" target="_blank">RSS: Click Here</a></i></li><li><i>For Non-Fiction writing by <a href="http://www.darrellwolfe.com/" target="_blank">Darrell Wolfe: Click Here</a></i></li></ul><ul></ul>
